summary: Comprehensive research on ADHD task management reveals core executive function deficits (time blindness, working memory, self-motivation, cognitive flexibility) and identifies evidence-based strategies. Most effective approaches: body doubling (social presence), visual time management, task breaking, behavioral momentum, external structure, and CBT techniques. Traditional to-do lists fail due to overwhelm, vagueness, lack of review, shame focus, and reliance on internal time sense.
key_findings: ADHD is fundamentally an executive function disorder affecting 6 clusters: activation, focus, effort, emotion, memory, and action
Time blindness is a neurological deficit linked to prefrontal cortex activity and default mode network connectivity, not a character flaw
Body doubling is among the most effective ADHD productivity strategies - activates accountability circuits and reduces distraction
Up to 60% of children with ADHD continue experiencing symptoms into adulthood, with cognitive impairments more prevalent than in childhood
CBT combined with medication is significantly more effective than medication alone for reducing symptoms and improving executive function
Traditional to-do lists fail for ADHD because they overwhelm, lack time externalization, focus on what's NOT done, and don't provide adequate scaffolding
design_implications: Make time visible and tangible through visual timers, progress bars, countdowns, and time-aware notifications
Support body doubling features: presence indicators, shared task lists, 'join session' functionality, progress sharing
Enforce task granularity: minimum viable size (≤30 min), clear completion criteria, automatic breaking of large tasks
Externalize motivation: completion celebrations, streak tracking, dopamine menus, gamified rewards
Reduce cognitive load: smart prioritization, context-aware suggestions, template libraries, quick capture
Incorporate CBT principles: reframed language (avoid 'should'), growth-focused messaging, shame interruption, implementation intentions
Address time blindness explicitly: always ask for time estimates, show cumulative time, buffer suggestions, urgency curves
Flexible but structured: multiple list views, drag-and-drop prioritization, time blocking integration, 'maybe later' list
sources: https://arxiv.org/html/2509.12153v1 - Designing Body Doubling for ADHD in VR
notes_for_orchestrator: No prior learnings found (build started fresh). Research strongly supports body doubling as a core feature - this should be prioritized in design. Time blindness is the fundamental deficit that task systems must address - traditional lists fail because they don't externalize time perception. Consider D1.2 should explore specific technical implementations of body doubling features and visual time management. The role of AI body doubles vs. human body doubles could be explored further - research shows both are effective.
